title: The Entente Strategy: How Democracies Could Lock In an AI Advantage

source.original: Machines of Loving Grace — Dario Amodei

body_html:
Unlike health or economic development, there's no structural reason to expect powerful AI to automatically favor democracy over authoritarianism. AI can supercharge propaganda and surveillance just as easily as it can accelerate drug discovery. The geopolitical implications of this are stark: if authoritarian regimes gain parity or superiority in AI capabilities, the consequences for human rights and global stability could be catastrophic.

One proposed approach to this challenge is an "entente strategy," in which a coalition of democracies works to establish a decisive—even if temporary—lead in AI capabilities. This would involve securing the AI supply chain (particularly chips and semiconductor equipment), scaling rapidly, and limiting adversaries' access to critical resources. The coalition would use this advantage both as a stick (robust military superiority) and a carrot (distributing AI benefits to countries that support democratic norms), gradually expanding the coalition and isolating the most authoritarian holdouts.

The historical analogy is something like "Atoms for Peace" meets Cold War containment—except the goal isn't just deterrence but the creation of conditions where authoritarian competitors find it more attractive to join the coalition than to fight it. If successful, this could produce a durable equilibrium where democracies set the global terms for how powerful AI is developed and deployed.

Within countries, a democratic AI advantage could also help tilt the internal balance toward freedom. If democracies control superior AI, they can counter foreign propaganda operations and provide uncensorable channels of information that authoritarian governments lack the technical ability to block. Historically, repressive regimes survive partly by controlling common knowledge—preventing citizens from collectively recognizing that "the emperor has no clothes." An AI-powered toolkit for dissidents, one that dictators cannot suppress, could provide a structural advantage to reformers everywhere.

None of this is inevitable. It requires deliberate coordination between AI companies and democratic governments, wise calibration of incentives, and sustained political will. But the framework suggests that the question of who leads in AI may be among the most consequential geopolitical decisions of the coming decade—and that passivity is the worst possible strategy.
